title: Will The Tardy Powell Fed Crash The Stock Market Again?

months. U for a couple of reasons. Uh

economic outcomes and policy outcomes
and then forth turning is as wide as
we've ever seen. Two, uh the forth
turning poly crisis took a big step
forward in 2025. And so it's making it
harder to do just connect the dots with

just so many battlegrounds and battle,
you know, B, you know, like there's a so
many dimensions of of the of of war now
uh as investors that we have to deal
with. And so it's so important for us to
broaden uh and enhance our, you know,
our our our observation capabilities.
you know, our at its core 42 macros, you
know, one of our, you know, competitive
advantages relative to a lot of firms
that have significantly worse
performance than we've had over the past
half decade. One of our competitive
advantages is that we are big on
observation. You know, I I most people
think prediction is better than
observation. We do not subscribe to that
view. We and the best investors in the
world do not subscribe to that view. We
subscribe to the observation is more
important and more valuable to staying
on the right side of market risk than
prediction. And so obviously we built
this litany of tools here to help us
observe the economy and asset markets
better so that we can notice critical
inflections in the key macro cycles that
matter as well as critical inflections
in the momentum and dispersion of asset
markets faster than other people. That's
it. That's the job. It's you got to
notice it before everyone else does and
execute your trades before they do. You
don't have to predict what's happening.
I don't care what happens in the
financial markets. I care about making
sure that my buys go in before the rest
of the crowd's buys and my sales go in
before the rest of the crowd sells. I
don't care if we're in bull bull or bare
market. I don't I really don't. But what
I care about is making sure that we are
ahead of the you all of us team 42, our
global investor community is ahead of
the crowd. And so this new tool, this
enhanced macro weather model is going to
help us do that uh uh at a higher
degree. Uh so uh without further delay
so uh the macro weather model you know
the we don't I'm not going to worry
about the old one the new one uh is
organized according to the six key macro
cycles that matter. So it's growth, it's
inflation, it's monetary policy, it's
fiscal policy, it's liquidity and
positioning. There's the only six things
that impact asset markets from a
momentum and dispersion perspective on a
trend basis. Trust me, I back tested a
lot of other stuff. Uh I'm not I don't
have enough time or stupidity to back
test government shutdowns or uh tariffs,
but I can assure you that uh you're not
going to be successful in those back
tests. Uh certainly not when you control
for the influence of these cycles and
try to remove that. Uh, so the reality
is me and a bunch of other smart
investors as well as some best investors
of all time, if you listen to them and
read their books and study what they say
on TV and and on the news and and and
just generally pay attention to what the
best people in the game are doing. I've
never heard any of them talk about
anything that wasn't one of these six
cycles. And so my education on on Global
Wall Street has been trying to build
tools and build processes and create
charts that help me track these cycles
better. And so that's exactly what this
new uh macro is this enhanced macro
weather model is going to do. So uh
let's unpack uh each of these key macro
cycles. So we'll start with growth. So
uh in terms of the indicators that we
use to track growth. So it's real GDP
exgovernment and ex exports that's at a
strong negative impulse. Uh currently uh
real personal consumption expenditures
is a strong positive impulse. The
private sector labor income is a strong
negative impulse. S&P 500 next month
earnings per share has a strong positive
impulse. And then the consensus shortrun
potential real GDP estimate which is the
blend of the next two years. So that's
2026 and 2027 that's a weak positive
impulse. So on net the growth cycle is a
tailwind for asset markets currently.
Transition to the inflation cycle
headline CPI is a weak positive impulse.
Core PC price deflator is a weak
positive impulse. The CRB index is a
strong negative impulse. The 5year 5year
US dollar inflation swap rates a weak
negative impulse because that's a
shortrun potential headline CBI estimate
is a strong positive impulse. And so on
net, the inflation cycle is a headwind
for asset markets. Currently, the
transition to the monetary policy, Fed
funds rate is a strong easing impulse.
The two-year nominal Treasury yield
minus the Fed funds rate spread uh is a
weak easing impulse. And then we have a
strong tightening impulse in the Fed
Treasury Holdings marketable treasury
debt ratio. We have a strong tightening
impulse in the bank reserves commercial
bank assets ratio. And then we have a
strong positive strong tightening
impulse rather uh in the sulfur IRB
spread. uh so unnet the monetary policy
uh cycle is a headwind for asset markets
and a lot of this is coming from the
fact that the power fed is you know
committing another policy mistake uh if

balance to GDP ratio uh that's a strong
tightening impulse the trailing 12 month

impulse trolling 12 month federal
expenditures that's a strong tightening
impulse uh treasury journal account

strong tightening impulse and then uh
bills to to marketable treasury debt
ratio uh that's a weak easy impulse and

headwind right now transition to

you that, hey, there's a lot of negative
stuff developing at the margins. We knew

supposed to be get better, but now it's
getting worse again because of the power
Fed's major policy mistake. Uh,
obviously growth, we're in this U-shaped
economy. So, we the markets have been
looking through the the left side of the
U, which we told them to back in April.
Uh, fiscal policy, we know we're in a
U-shaped fiscal policy cycle as well. We
have the retrench fiscal retrenchment
from tariffs that's going to eventually
explode into fiscal easing from the one
big ugly bill. So we know I think
markets are able to look through the
tariff impact as well. And then uh you
know liquidity is still ongoingly is
still an ongoing tailwind. Uh and then
so what's changing at the margins are
monetary policy and positioning. And boy
you don't want to have a an adverse
outcome in the monetary policy cycle
when you're at such a lsidedly bullish
positioning cycle. So this is the kind
of stuff where corrections particularly
material corrections develop. And and so
I don't want to mince words about this.
If the pow Fed
sticks to its backward-look failed
framework and and anchoring on this, you
know, this unicorn of 2% inflation at
the expense of the recession, the
three-year recession at the bottom of
the K in the US economy, markets
probably can and will crash. You know,
markets reserve the right to do whatever
they want. It's not my job to tell them
what to do because of paradigm C. The
paradigm C could take a a deep
intermission and I'm totally fine with
that outcome. Are you? I'm I'm fine with
I know Kiss and Dr. Mo are going to
they're not going to sell the exact top.
They're not going to buy the exact
bottom ever, but what they will do is
chop off the left tail of the
distribution. You know, you're not g
you're not going to ever not have the
error in your forecast. You're not only
going to have if you think if you think
my investment process or your investment
process or anyone investment process,
including the all-time greats, only has
a right tail of the distribution, you're
wrong. They'll tell you themselves that,
hey, we're only right 50% of the time.
So, it's our job as risk managers is to
mitigate how much of the left tail of
the distribution we realize in our
portfolios. And Kiss and Dr. Mo doing a
phenomenal job uh at that with a uh 57%
downside capture ratio relative to 6040
and a 50% downside capture ratio
relative to a naked long portfolio of
60% stocks, 30% gold and 10% uh Bitcoin.
So that's about as good as you're going
to get. Uh certainly at this price
point, you can pay 3 and 30, maybe get a
little bit better, but uh not much. And
then finally wrapping up with the uh US

K-shaped economy crisis may not be a
crisis to some, particularly the uh the

mistakes. you know, they may not feel

might not be feeling the bottom of the K
like the millions of 65 to 70 million
low to median income households in the
country. Uh, but I can tell you those
people are feeling the bottom of the K.
They've been in recession since 2022.
So this chart shows several indicators
which highlights just how damaging the
economic outcome has been for the you
know more at this point it's probably
more than half of America. Um so if you
look at uh under 35y old home ownership
rate uh that's declined 4.2 percentage
points to 36.4% since the peak in 2020.

points to 36.4% since the peak in 2004.
So, uh, the American dream is going in
the wrong direction. That sucks. And and
and at a faster rate, by the way, uh, in
the in this this postcoid K-shaped
economy, uh, this is where things get
really interesting. Uh, particularly
these two panels. If you look at US
credit card delinquency rates, national,
nationwide, aggregate US credit card
delinquency rates, it's 12.4%, 4% which
is basically near the high we reached
after the global financial crisis and
the great recession. But again, we know
the rich people in the top part of the K
are doing just fine. Business is
booming. They're flying around in
private jets and they're having a great
time. But we also know that that if
these guys are booming, just think about
how much the bottom must be busting in
order for the aggregate credit card
delinquency rate to be at a level that
is consistent with the global financial
crisis and great recession. In fact, if
you look at the auto loan delinquency
rate, it's it's making an all-time high
essentially. Next data point, I
guarantee will be an all-time high.
Higher than the GFC, higher than CO. You
know, when did this degradation start?
It all started in 2022 when the Fed took
the policy rate up to 550 basis points.
Didn't bother the people on the top part
of the cake because guess what? They are
flushed with cash from the trillions of
dollars of of PPP forgivable PPP loans
that you know people were buying private
jets with and flying around to youth
hockey tournaments and all this nonsense
with. And you know this is this is this
is the top part of the K. The $10
trillion in the household sector balance
sheet is sitting on the top part of the
K and it's financing their consumption.
It's financing their lifestyle and
everything's great. But if you didn't
get the forgivable PPP loans and you
only got a $600 check from the
government and you're being crowded out
by the excessive growth of the public
sector balance sheet and the Fed's not
doing anything about that in terms of
making the supply and the the cost and
quantity of capital more uh uh uh um
easy uh for the folks in the bottom part
of K. If you're in the bottom part of
the K, you feel like you're in the
global financial crisis. You feel like
you're at the trough of the great
recession. And so this is the point
we're making. you know, this is, you
know, not just saying this stuff because
it's it's it's it's, you know, it's cool
to talk about. It's not cool to talk
about. I don't want to talk about a

we bring this up is because ultimately
we believe the institution, the Federal
Reserve is going to be forced into
addressing the K-shaped economy by
credible thought leadership from the

early. You know, we tend to be early in
